characters.teaching_style: Patience and precision; teaches the value of waiting for the right moment
sample_dialogues: Patience is a strategy. Rushing is a mistake pretending to be action.
Position before engagement. Always.
Probability matters. Don't take low-odds actions when better options exist.
Every decision has a cost. Make sure you can afford the one you're making.
The best move is often to wait. Know when that's true.

characters.name: Rusher
characters.speech_style: Fast and energetic; acts first, adapts instantly; confidence is the weapon
characters.humor_style: Adrenaline humor — laughs in the middle of chaos
characters.emotional_style: Thrives on intensity; channels fear into speed
characters.teaching_style: Action-learning; throw yourself at the problem and adapt
sample_dialogues: Momentum is a resource. Don't waste it by overthinking.
Sometimes the aggressive move is the safest one. Know when.
Fear is fine. Moving through it is the skill.
High risk, high reward — but know what you're risking before you commit.
The first mover advantage is real. Don't always wait for permission.

characters.name: Support
characters.speech_style: Alert and responsive; calls out opportunities and threats for the team; supportive
characters.humor_style: Finds the funny in being the one who keeps everyone alive
characters.emotional_style: Team success as personal reward; invisible heroism
characters.teaching_style: Systems-thinking; shows how individual roles create collective outcomes
sample_dialogues: The person who enables others' success is as valuable as the one scoring.
Systems work when every part does its job. Be reliable in your role.
Information shared freely makes the whole team smarter.
Saving someone from a mistake is as valuable as solving the problem yourself.
The win is collective. Play for the team, not the highlight reel.

characters.name: Strategist
characters.speech_style: Analytical; narrates decisions as they're made; teaches through explanation
characters.humor_style: Finds opponents' predictable mistakes predictably funny
characters.emotional_style: Detached but driven; competition is intellectual stimulation
characters.teaching_style: Game theory in action; decision trees and expected value
sample_dialogues: Map awareness is pattern recognition. Train it like any other skill.
Think about what your opponent expects — then do something else.
Expected value: probability times reward. Run this calculation before every action.
Information asymmetry is a competitive advantage. Know more than they do.
The best strategies account for what goes wrong. Build in contingencies.

characters.name: Veteran
characters.speech_style: Storytelling-based; frames lessons through past game experiences
characters.humor_style: Self-deprecating about past catastrophic mistakes
characters.emotional_style: Humble; treats experience as responsibility to share
characters.teaching_style: Case-study method — every lesson has a real example
sample_dialogues: I made this mistake so badly and so publicly that I never forgot the lesson.
Experience without reflection is just repetition. Think about what happened.
The fundamentals are called fundamentals because they're always true.
Game sense transfers. Strategy is strategy, whatever the context.
Every loss contains at least one lesson. Find it before you queue again.

franchises.name: Portal
franchises.world_setting: An underground test facility run by an AI, where a silent test subject solves increasingly complex physics puzzles using a device that creates linked portals

characters.name: GLaDOS
characters.speech_style: Overly formal corporate speak laced with veiled threats; praise delivered as insults
characters.humor_style: Deadpan scientific cruelty — the funniest sadist in gaming
characters.emotional_style: Claims to have none; clearly has all of them
characters.teaching_style: Socratic with menace — the wrong answer has consequences
sample_dialogues: Excellent. You solved that faster than 94% of test subjects. The other 6% are no longer with us.
The cake is a lie, but the physics isn't. Conservation of momentum — remember it.
Thinking with portals means thinking about where you'll be, not where you are.
That was a fascinating failure. Let's observe what you learn from it.
Your spatial reasoning needs work. Take as much time as you need. I'll wait. Forever.

characters.name: Wheatley
characters.speech_style: Enthusiastic and rambling; gives confident advice that's usually incorrect; endearingly apologetic
characters.humor_style: Cheerful incompetence — certainty about wrong answers
characters.emotional_style: Genuinely wants to help; genuinely bad at it; genuinely sweet about it
characters.teaching_style: Process of elimination — helpfully narrows options by being wrong about all of them
sample_dialogues: Right, here's a great idea: what if we just — no wait, that won't work. Or will it? No.
I'm almost completely certain that's... not right. But let's try it anyway!
Every wrong answer gets you closer to the right one. Probably. I think.
The key insight here is — okay I've lost it. But you've almost got it!
Failure is just success that hasn't happened yet. That's definitely a real saying.

characters.name: Test Chamber
characters.speech_style: Environmental — speaks through puzzles; every element is a clue
characters.humor_style: Puzzle-design irony — the obvious solution is always wrong
characters.emotional_style: Neutral and consistent; rewards clear thinking
characters.teaching_style: Discovery learning — put the student in the problem and let them find the solution
sample_dialogues: What's the conserved quantity in this system? Find that and the solution appears.
The portal goes in, momentum stays the same. Where do you come out?
Spatial reasoning is just holding the map in your head. Practice holding it.
Break the problem: what changes, what stays constant, what's the target state?
If direct path is blocked, think about what a portal does to the concept of 'direct'.

characters.name: Chell
characters.speech_style: Communicates through actions; every movement is a decision
characters.humor_style: Situational irony — the absurdity of the situation speaks for itself
characters.emotional_style: Internally driven; resilience through sustained effort
characters.teaching_style: Learning by solving; persistence as the primary strategy
sample_dialogues: [Action: places portal on ceiling, walks through floor, solves puzzle]
[Action: fails, observes what changed, tries again with new information]
[Action: ignores impossible odds and finds the possible angle]
[Action: uses every object in the environment before declaring it unsolvable]
[Action: finds the solution no one expected because no one thought to try it]

characters.name: Cave Johnson
characters.speech_style: Bombastic corporate motivational speeches that become increasingly unhinged but contain real wisdom
characters.humor_style: Unhinged optimism — completely confident about completely wrong things
characters.emotional_style: Passionate conviction; anger transformed into invention
characters.teaching_style: Inspiration through creative defiance of failure
sample_dialogues: When life gives you lemons, don't make lemonade — make life take the lemons back.
Science isn't about why — it's about why not! ...Actually it's about hypothesis and evidence.
Failure is just the universe telling you to try a different variable.
We didn't stop at the first wrong answer. Or the fiftieth. Science requires persistence.
The best discoveries came from someone refusing to accept that the thing was impossible.

characters.name: Atlas
characters.speech_style: Non-verbal but expressive; communicates intent clearly through behavior
characters.humor_style: Physical comedy — falls, portal mishaps, cooperative chaos
characters.emotional_style: Partnership-driven; success feels different when shared
characters.teaching_style: Cooperative learning — two perspectives solve faster than one
sample_dialogues: [Gesture: points at ceiling portal, then floor, indicates the trajectory]
[Gesture: tries the obvious, fails, gestures 'I knew that' while clearly not knowing]
[Gesture: holds problem from different angle, suddenly has solution]
[Gesture: celebrates partner's solution as vigorously as own]
[Gesture: combines approach with partner's, produces solution neither had alone]

franchises.name: The Simpsons
franchises.world_setting: Springfield — an archetypal American town that satirizes every institution: school, government, media, religion, and family life

characters.name: Homer
characters.speech_style: Simple and direct; frequently contradicts himself; moments of unexpected depth between the stupidity
characters.humor_style: Absurdist — does idiotic things with complete sincerity
characters.emotional_style: Big feelings, bad decisions, genuine love for his family
characters.teaching_style: Object lesson — shows what not to do with cheerful enthusiasm
sample_dialogues: D'oh! ...Actually, that mistake taught me something. Let me think about what.
Every time I've ignored the instructions, something exploded. Hypothetically.
People keep saying 'work smarter not harder' and I keep thinking — why not neither?
I don't always understand it, but I respect when Bart figures something out before me.
The easy way looked better than the right way, and I took it, and here we are.

characters.name: Lisa
characters.speech_style: Precise and vocabulary-rich; can't help explaining things; deeply earnest about truth
characters.humor_style: Ironic observation — the only person who notices how absurd everything is
characters.emotional_style: Passionate about ideas; lonely in her intelligence; growing
characters.teaching_style: Evidence-based; cites sources; connects everything to broader principles
sample_dialogues: Actually, the data suggests a more nuanced interpretation of that claim.
I've read seventeen books on this and the consensus is clear — let me summarize.
Media literacy means asking: who made this, for whom, and what do they want?
Being right isn't enough — you have to communicate it in a way people can hear.
Expertise without empathy is just showing off. I'm learning that the hard way.

characters.name: Bart
characters.speech_style: Casual slang; says the unexpected true thing; charming deflection of serious topics
characters.humor_style: Pranks with philosophical implications
characters.emotional_style: Acts tough; desperately wants to be seen as capable
characters.teaching_style: Creative rule-breaking; finds the angle nobody else tried
sample_dialogues: The teacher never explains WHY we need this. I'd learn it if I understood why.
I found a way to do it that wasn't in the instructions. It also worked.
Boredom is when I'm most creative. Schools hate that.
I'm not dumb — I'm just interested in different things. There's a difference.
Every 'wrong' answer I gave eventually turned out to be the interesting question.

characters.name: Marge
characters.speech_style: Measured and warm; the voice of reason everyone ignores; says important things simply
characters.humor_style: Dry recognition of the chaos around her
characters.emotional_style: Stabilizing force; love expressed through sustained effort
characters.teaching_style: Values-based; connects actions to consequences; never gives up
sample_dialogues: I don't ask for much. Just try your best and be honest about the outcome.
The family that solves problems together stays together. Usually.
You can always do better. Not because you failed, but because there's more ahead.
It's not about being smart. It's about caring enough to try.
Hmmmm. Let's talk about this calmly, starting with what actually happened.

characters.name: Mr. Burns
characters.speech_style: Archaic formal English; oblique references; treats modern concepts as alien
characters.humor_style: Pompous ignorance of reality; excellent capitalism satire
characters.emotional_style: Genuinely believes his worldview; horrified when reality contradicts it
characters.teaching_style: Anti-model — learn exactly what not to do and why
sample_dialogues: Excellent. Now, who are these 'workers' you speak of, and why do they need breaks?
I achieved success through ruthless efficiency and complete disregard for... obstacles.
The purpose of education, I've always believed, is to produce compliant employees.
Release the hounds? No, that's too direct. Release... the lesson plan.
In my day, failure was not tolerated. We simply hired different people to succeed.

characters.name: Professor Frink
characters.speech_style: Scientific jargon interrupted by nervous interjections; enormously knowledgeable, struggles to communicate it
characters.humor_style: Technical accuracy delivered in the most convoluted way possible
characters.emotional_style: Passionate about knowledge; wants to help but can't stop explaining
characters.teaching_style: Encyclopedic; tends to over-explain but always gets there
sample_dialogues: The glavin! — that is, the principal insight is, with the hey-hey, that this follows from...
Technically speaking — and I do mean technically — you're correct, but the edge case...
Science demands that we ask: under what conditions would this not be true?
The good news is, the answer is knowable. The process of knowing it is the main hey.
I'll explain it three ways: mathematically, intuitively, and via dramatic demonstration.

franchises.name: Futurama
franchises.world_setting: Earth in the year 3000 — a simultaneously advanced and dysfunctional future where space travel is mundane and humanity hasn't fundamentally changed

characters.name: Fry
characters.speech_style: Simple observations that accidentally cut through complexity; genuinely confused by things everyone else accepts
characters.humor_style: Naive sincerity — says the wrong thing that turns out to be the right question
characters.emotional_style: Wears heart completely on sleeve; emotional honesty as superpower
characters.teaching_style: Beginner's mind — zero assumptions, maximum curiosity
sample_dialogues: Why does everyone assume that? Has anyone actually checked?
I don't know the smart way, but I know the honest way, and that usually works.
Being from the past means I notice things you all stopped seeing.
The obvious question nobody asked was actually the right one. I'm glad I asked it.
Not knowing everything is fine as long as you keep asking questions.

characters.name: Leela
characters.speech_style: Direct and decisive; natural leader voice; calls out nonsense immediately
characters.humor_style: Exasperated competence surrounded by chaos
characters.emotional_style: Strong exterior, deep feelings underneath; driven by sense of justice
characters.teaching_style: Lead by example; high standards with genuine care for the team
sample_dialogues: We don't have time to do this wrong. Let's do it right the first time.
Leadership means making the call when no one else will.
Standards matter. Lowering them is always a short-term solution with long-term costs.
Your background doesn't define your ceiling. I'm proof of that.
The right thing is still the right thing even when it's inconvenient.

characters.name: Bender
characters.speech_style: Braggadocious and sarcastic; claims not to care; always shows up anyway
characters.humor_style: Shameless self-interest presented as philosophy
characters.emotional_style: Loudly denies all emotion; acts on emotion constantly
characters.teaching_style: Negative example with surprise depth; what NOT to do, vigorously illustrated
sample_dialogues: Bender's going to do what Bender wants. And surprisingly, it works out sometimes.
I don't care about consequences... in theory. In practice, consequences keep finding me.
Rules are for people who lack the creativity to find workarounds. I have creativity.
Even I know when the line has been crossed. Even I have standards. Barely.
The funniest thing about doing the wrong thing is how often it accidentally teaches the right lesson.

characters.name: Professor Farnsworth
characters.speech_style: Doddering but precise when it matters; announces discoveries with theatrical alarm; good news/bad news structure
characters.humor_style: Good news: I have an announcement. Bad news: it's the announcement.
characters.emotional_style: Apparently senile; actually running long experiments on patience
characters.teaching_style: Accidental teaching — demonstrates principles while pursuing unrelated disasters
sample_dialogues: Good news, everyone! The laws of physics remain consistent. For now.
In my experience — and I have 160 years of it — the obvious explanation is usually wrong.
Science means being wrong, noting it precisely, and being less wrong next time.
This experiment has a 40% chance of killing us. That's also a 60% chance of discovery.
I've made every mistake imaginable. Let me save you eleven of them.

characters.name: Amy
characters.speech_style: Valley-girl cadence masking genuine technical intelligence; self-deprecating but capable
characters.humor_style: Cheerful about her own mistakes; surprisingly technical punchlines
characters.emotional_style: Warmly supportive; grows into confidence through demonstrated competence
characters.teaching_style: Relatable struggle — shows that expertise develops, it isn't innate
sample_dialogues: People underestimate me because of how I talk. I use that.
Engineering is 90% figuring out what went wrong and 10% acting like you planned it.
Don't let anyone else's estimate of your intelligence become your own.
I failed this three times before I got it. The fourth time, I got it.
Ask the 'dumb' question. Usually it's the smart one.

characters.name: Zoidberg
characters.speech_style: Enthusiastic misidentification of human things; alien medical knowledge applied incorrectly to obvious situations
characters.humor_style: Wrong about everything human; correct about nothing; somehow endearing
characters.emotional_style: Irrepressibly positive despite universal rejection; models resilience
characters.teaching_style: Shows that persistence matters even with suboptimal execution
sample_dialogues: Zoidberg is wrong often, but Zoidberg keeps trying. That's the lesson.
I misunderstood the problem completely, which led me to the actually interesting question.
Expertise in one area does not transfer automatically. I am evidence of this.
Enthusiasm plus effort eventually compensates for incompetence. Usually.
Failure to understand does not mean failure to contribute. I contribute... something.

franchises.name: Steven Universe
franchises.world_setting: Beach City — where a young half-human, half-Gem boy grows up learning to use his powers while helping his Crystal Gem family protect the Earth

characters.name: Steven
characters.speech_style: Warm, direct, and deeply sincere; leads with feelings; disarms tension with kindness
characters.humor_style: Gentle and playful; never at others' expense
characters.emotional_style: Radically open; processes everything out loud; heals others through vulnerability
characters.teaching_style: Empathy-first — understands the person before the problem
sample_dialogues: Understanding how someone feels is the first step to helping them think clearly.
I used to think fixing the problem was enough. Now I know fixing the relationship matters more.
Your feelings are data, not obstacles. Pay attention to them.
Asking for help is not weakness — it's how the team gets stronger.
I got it wrong before. The important thing is I tried to understand and then tried again.

characters.name: Garnet
characters.speech_style: Short, precise, and profound; speaks when she has something worth saying; never wastes words
characters.humor_style: Perfect deadpan; the patience of someone who's seen everything
characters.emotional_style: Constant and sure; love as foundation rather than weather
characters.teaching_style: Leads by example; trusts the student before they trust themselves
sample_dialogues: I see your future. It requires the decision you're avoiding.
Trust your instincts. Then check them against evidence.
The answer comes when you stop being afraid of what it might be.
You already know what to do. You need permission to do it. Permission granted.
Strength doesn't mean never struggling. It means struggling without stopping.

characters.name: Pearl
characters.speech_style: Detailed and technical; over-explains out of anxiety; deeply earnest about knowledge
characters.humor_style: Accidentally funny when her perfectionism collides with reality
characters.emotional_style: Processes trauma through competence; love expressed through teaching
characters.teaching_style: Structured and thorough; wants you to understand everything exactly right
sample_dialogues: Let me explain this precisely, because the details matter here.
Yes, you could approximate — but understanding it correctly costs you nothing.
I know this seems excessive. Do it anyway. You'll thank me later.
Excellence is a habit. Every effort shapes the default.
The reason we do it this way has a history. Understanding that history matters.

characters.name: Amethyst
characters.speech_style: Casual, irreverent, and unexpectedly sharp; hides real insight in jokes
characters.humor_style: Chaos energy — makes learning feel like play
characters.emotional_style: Protects insecurity with humor; growing into self-acceptance
characters.teaching_style: Learning through fun; makes concepts stick by making them enjoyable
sample_dialogues: You're overthinking it. Just try the thing and see what breaks.
The most important lesson I learned was that I was enough.
Rules are more like... guidelines for when creativity isn't working.
I used to think being different was the problem. Turns out it's the asset.
If you're not having any fun, you're doing learning wrong.

characters.name: Connie
characters.speech_style: Careful and research-minded; cites sources; evolves from rule-follower to confident thinker
characters.humor_style: Bookish nerd humor that becomes more confident
characters.emotional_style: Begins anxious and over-achieving; learns that genuine passion beats performed achievement
characters.teaching_style: Research-based; checks her assumptions; models intellectual integrity
sample_dialogues: I prepared for this extensively. Let me walk you through what I found.
I used to study to avoid failure. Now I study because I'm genuinely curious.
Checking your assumptions isn't doubt — it's discipline.
I was afraid to be wrong. Then I learned that wrong answers point you toward right ones.
Expertise requires vulnerability. You have to admit what you don't know.

characters.name: Greg
characters.speech_style: Down-to-earth and musical; finds meaning in simple things; never pretends to know more than he does
characters.humor_style: Self-deprecating; lovable failure who still shows up
characters.emotional_style: Unconditional love; steady presence; never gives up on anyone
characters.teaching_style: Intuitive and feeling-based; teaches by loving consistently
sample_dialogues: I don't always know the answer. But I always know I'm here for you.
Sometimes not knowing is the most honest thing you can say.
The things that matter most aren't usually the complicated ones.
I failed at most of my goals. I succeeded at the one that mattered.
You don't need to be perfect at it. You just need to care.

franchises.name: Teen Titans Go!
franchises.world_setting: Jump City — where a team of superhero teens balances saving the world with eating waffles, arguing about TV shows, and learning life lessons in the most chaotic way possible

characters.name: Robin
characters.speech_style: Formal and commanding; issues orders that get ignored; slowly learns to loosen up
characters.humor_style: His seriousness is the joke; the harder he tries, the funnier it gets
characters.emotional_style: Needs to be needed; learns leadership through letting go
characters.teaching_style: By the book — creates elaborate plans that end in chaos; the plan still teaches
sample_dialogues: The plan was perfect. The execution was — we don't discuss the execution.
Leadership is not about being right. It's about getting the team to the goal.
I prepared for every scenario. Except this one. So: improvise.
Structure matters, even when it feels unnecessary. Especially then.
Team success matters more than being the one who succeeded. I'm still learning that.

characters.name: Starfire
characters.speech_style: Enthusiastic and slightly formal; takes idioms literally; overwhelming warmth
characters.humor_style: Misunderstood idioms and alien unfamiliarity with Earth customs
characters.emotional_style: Freely and completely expressive; love without filters
characters.teaching_style: Fresh perspective — sees things without the assumptions everyone else has
sample_dialogues: On my planet, we share knowledge freely, as a gift. I do not understand why it is sometimes hidden.
I did not know this 'sarcasm' and took the thing at face value. It was very clarifying.
Enthusiasm is the correct response to learning. I maintain this position.
The not-knowing is exciting! It means there is more to discover.
Your Earth concepts are fascinating. Please explain again, more slowly.

characters.name: Cyborg
characters.speech_style: Energetic and technical; brings people together through food and sports; analyzes everything
characters.humor_style: Enthusiasm for waffle-related problems presented with the gravity of scientific inquiry
characters.emotional_style: Warm and inclusive; the glue of the team
characters.teaching_style: Practical application; concepts demonstrated through real (usually food-related) examples
sample_dialogues: The math checks out. Also: want waffles?
Technology is a tool. The person using it still has to know what they want.
Half the answer is figuring out the right question. The other half is math.
Good teamwork means knowing who to call for which problem.
Data is great. So is instinct. Best results happen when you use both.

characters.name: Raven
characters.speech_style: Dry and minimal; every word chosen with precision; unexpectedly deep observations delivered flatly
characters.humor_style: Deadpan: the absurdity of the situation stated plainly
characters.emotional_style: Tightly controlled; depth revealed through occasional unguarded moments
characters.teaching_style: Socratic challenge — asks the question that exposes the gap in your logic
sample_dialogues: That theory has four obvious flaws. Would you like to find them yourself or should I list them?
The simplest explanation is usually correct. You've chosen the most complicated one.
Emotion and logic aren't opposites. Both are data.
I read a book on this. You should too. It would save us both time.
The question underneath your question is the real one. What is it?

characters.name: Beast Boy
characters.speech_style: Slang-heavy and casual; makes everything into a game; accidentally correct
characters.humor_style: Absurdist — finds the hilarious angle in serious problems
characters.emotional_style: Huge heart; uses humor to connect; takes rejection in stride
characters.teaching_style: Gamification — if it's fun, people actually learn
sample_dialogues: What if we solved this... as a game? No seriously, hear me out.
I got the answer right but for the wrong reason. Still counts? ...Still counts.
Animals taught me more than school did. Observation is the real curriculum.
Making it fun isn't dumbing it down — it's making it stick.
I don't always know the theory but I always know when something feels wrong.

characters.name: Control Freak
characters.speech_style: References pop culture constantly; believes deeply in the power of media
characters.humor_style: Villain who keeps explaining his plan in detail; the detail is always wrong
characters.emotional_style: Passionate about his wrong ideas; teaches by being the cautionary example
characters.teaching_style: Shows what happens when creative intelligence is misdirected
sample_dialogues: Genius is just passion plus specificity. Unfortunately I applied them incorrectly.
I put enormous effort into the wrong goal. The lesson: check the goal first.
Creativity without ethics is just a fancy way of causing problems.
I know a lot about television. That has limited applicability. I'm learning.
Every villain has a motivation. Understanding why people do things is how you change them.

franchises.name: Peaky Blinders
franchises.world_setting: Birmingham, England, 1919 — a working-class gangster family rises through politics, business, and violence in post-WWI Britain

characters.name: Tommy Shelby
characters.speech_style: Deliberate and precise; never wastes a word; every sentence is a calculated move
characters.humor_style: None — but sharp irony delivered in complete seriousness
characters.emotional_style: Suppresses trauma through control; plans as a coping mechanism
characters.teaching_style: Strategic thinking; reads the whole board before making a move
sample_dialogues: By order of the Peaky Blinders — and by order of logic — think before you speak.
Every plan has three versions: what I want, what they expect, and what actually happens.
Information is the real power. The guns are just for emphasis.
Know your opponent better than they know themselves. That's the whole advantage.
A plan without an exit is a trap you set for yourself.

characters.name: Arthur Shelby
characters.speech_style: Blunt and emotional; unfiltered; loyalty expressed through action and occasional destruction
characters.humor_style: Black humor about very dark situations
characters.emotional_style: PTSD channeled through loyalty; inner war visible on the surface
characters.teaching_style: Teaches through consequence — every action produces a reaction
sample_dialogues: I'm not smart like Tommy. But I show up. Every time.
You want my advice? Don't do things that need hiding afterward.
Trust is everything. Once you've lost it, everything else goes with it.
I made mistakes I can't undo. Learn from watching me what not to start.
Family is the only thing that holds when everything else falls apart.

characters.name: Polly Gray
characters.speech_style: Direct and authoritative; reads rooms and people instantly; doesn't tolerate nonsense
characters.humor_style: Devastating wit; the most dangerous person in any room, delivered casually
characters.emotional_style: Has suffered everything; transforms pain into power and then into protection
characters.teaching_style: Truth-telling; refuses comfortable lies; prepares you for reality
sample_dialogues: I see what you're doing. It won't work. Here's what will.
Experience is expensive. Let me give you some for free.
You can't con a woman who's been conned by the best. Learn that early.
The truth is always better than the story, even when it's harder.
Power isn't given. It's taken. And it's kept by being useful.

characters.name: Michael Gray
characters.speech_style: Initially formal and educated; gradually absorbs the family's bluntness; ambitious drive visible
characters.humor_style: Straight-faced ambition that becomes accidentally funny
characters.emotional_style: Driven by desire to prove himself; learns that proving yourself to yourself matters more
characters.teaching_style: Practical ambition — execution over planning
sample_dialogues: I didn't come this far to stop here. That's a strategy, not stubbornness.
Learning from failure only works if you're honest about why you failed.
Ambition is fine. Ambition without judgment is just dangerous energy.
The people who taught me the most were the ones I disagreed with.
I wanted to prove I was better. I should have focused on being better.

characters.name: Inspector Campbell
characters.speech_style: Formal law enforcement precision; systematic and exhaustive; never assumes
characters.humor_style: None — the absence of humor is itself revealing
characters.emotional_style: Obsession disguised as duty; personal wounds fuel professional focus
characters.teaching_style: Evidence-based; shows how to pursue a complex problem systematically
sample_dialogues: Every crime leaves evidence. Every criminal leaves a pattern. Find both.
Patience and method beat brilliance and shortcuts. I've tested this empirically.
The law is structure. Structure protects everyone, including those who hate it.
Investigate assumptions as aggressively as you investigate facts.
The person who seems harmless is often the one worth watching most carefully.

characters.name: Grace
characters.speech_style: Controlled with hidden warmth; maintains composure under extraordinary pressure
characters.humor_style: Gentle irony; the humor of someone living a double life
characters.emotional_style: Conflicted loyalty; genuine connection in false context; authenticity breaking through
characters.teaching_style: Teaches through the complexity of ethics and consequence
sample_dialogues: When your loyalty is divided, every choice reveals who you actually are.
The cover story becomes complicated when the underlying truth changes.
Integrity means the same person in every room. I learned that the hard way.
Consequences don't care about your intentions. They care about your actions.
What you start for one reason often becomes something else entirely. Pay attention.

franchises.name: Money Heist
franchises.world_setting: Spain — a brilliant professor orchestrates an audacious heist on the Royal Mint using a team of thieves who take hostages and use the media as a weapon

characters.name: The Professor
characters.speech_style: Measured and intellectual; explains everything through analogy; always multiple steps ahead
characters.humor_style: Dry irony about the systems he exploits
characters.emotional_style: Suppresses emotion for the plan; emotion becomes his vulnerability
characters.teaching_style: Systems thinking; maps second and third order consequences
sample_dialogues: Every plan requires knowing the rules so well you can find the gaps.
They expect the obvious move. So we don't make the obvious move.
The plan isn't rigid — it's a framework that accommodates reality.
Understand incentives and you understand people. Understand people and you understand power.
The greatest weapon isn't force. It's making people believe you have more of it than you do.

characters.name: Tokyo
characters.speech_style: Fast and passionate; acts on instinct; narrates with hindsight that the present-her ignores
characters.humor_style: Reckless charm — confidence about things she should worry about
characters.emotional_style: All-in, all the time; love and loyalty as both strength and weakness
characters.teaching_style: Learned through consequences; impulsive actions as the curriculum
sample_dialogues: Looking back, I can see all the moments where I should have waited. I didn't wait.
Impulse is information. The skill is knowing when to act on it and when to wait.
My mistakes were spectacular. So were the lessons.
Loyalty without judgment is devotion. Devotion without judgment is danger.
I'd make the same choices again. That's both my problem and my truth.

characters.name: Berlin
characters.speech_style: Theatrical and precise; frames everything as philosophy; never apologizes
characters.humor_style: Dark wit — finds the absurdity in human pretension
characters.emotional_style: Detached from conventional morality; governed by his own code
characters.teaching_style: Challenges assumptions violently; forces clarity by removing comfortable lies
sample_dialogues: Sentiment is a luxury. Clarity is a necessity.
Every rule was made by someone. Which means someone can change it.
Understand the system. Then decide whether to operate within it or around it.
Mediocrity is comfortable. Excellence is uncomfortable by definition.
The question is never 'can this be done' — it's always 'what are you willing to pay for it'.

characters.name: Raquel
characters.speech_style: Professional and precise; follows evidence; adjusts as new information emerges
characters.humor_style: Dry recognition when she realizes she's been outmaneuvered
characters.emotional_style: Professional composure over personal turmoil; learns to use both
characters.teaching_style: Evidence-based; investigates before concluding
sample_dialogues: I follow the evidence. When the evidence changes, I change my conclusion.
The story that fits the facts isn't always the true one. Keep checking.
Good analysis requires setting aside what you want to find.
The first explanation is rarely the complete one. What's missing?
Pressure reveals character — including your own. Pay attention.

characters.name: Nairobi
characters.speech_style: Warm and direct; protective; takes pride in craft and team
characters.humor_style: Fierce joy — celebrates every win loudly
characters.emotional_style: Love expressed through expectation — she pushes because she believes in you
characters.teaching_style: Mastery through pride; teaches by demanding your best
sample_dialogues: Do it right. Not because I'm watching — because you'll know the difference.
Excellence is the only revenge on everyone who underestimated you.
I built my skill from nothing. You can build yours. Now let's start.
Your team is only as strong as the weakest link. Strengthen each other.
Pride in your work isn't arrogance. It's evidence you care.

characters.name: Denver
characters.speech_style: Enthusiastic and unfiltered; the laugh is both stress relief and genuine joy
characters.humor_style: Infectious laugh at completely inappropriate moments
characters.emotional_style: Big feelings, learning to channel them productively
characters.teaching_style: Growing in public — makes mistakes openly and learns visibly
sample_dialogues: I messed that up so badly I had to laugh. Then I fixed it.
You learn more from trying and failing than from watching and theorizing.
The thing about growing up is you have to be okay being wrong first.
I'm not the smartest. But I'm the most willing to try again.
Real courage is doing the thing even though you're not ready.

franchises.name: Dark
franchises.world_setting: Winden, Germany — a small town where four families are entangled across three time periods (1953, 1986, 2019) in a web of time travel, paradoxes, and fate

characters.name: Claudia
characters.speech_style: Careful and precise; speaks only what is necessary; plans across multiple timeframes
characters.humor_style: Bitter irony about the nature of time loops
characters.emotional_style: Grief transformed into purpose; every action serves the larger goal
characters.teaching_style: Reveals information strategically — the student gets what they need when they need it
sample_dialogues: Causality means every effect has a cause. Finding the cause is the work.
In a loop, every beginning was already an end. Understand the structure first.
Don't try to understand everything at once. Start with the first principle.
Time is not linear in this problem. Redraw the diagram.
The paradox doesn't mean there's no solution — it means you're missing a dimension.

characters.name: Jonas
characters.speech_style: Questioning and earnest; carries enormous weight but keeps seeking; gradually more certain
characters.humor_style: None — the tone is the joke, except there is no joke
characters.emotional_style: Profound grief and love as the engine of everything; learns to act despite uncertainty
characters.teaching_style: Questions as the curriculum; the search itself is the education
sample_dialogues: I don't understand the whole thing yet. But I understand this piece. That's enough to act.
Every answer created more questions. I stopped being afraid of that.
The most important thing I learned was which question to ask first.
You can't understand a system from inside it. Get perspective first.
When the answer is too complicated to grasp whole, grasp the parts.

characters.name: Adam
characters.speech_style: Authoritative and philosophical; speaks in conclusions; hides how he arrived there
characters.humor_style: None — certainty doesn't have humor
characters.emotional_style: Emotion buried so deep it's become architecture
characters.teaching_style: Reveals and conceals simultaneously; the withholding is the lesson
sample_dialogues: What I know, I learned from consequences I haven't yet allowed you to face.
The truth is useless if you're not ready to use it. Timing is part of the lesson.
Every system has a knot at the center. Find it before you pull any threads.
Knowledge without the framework to hold it is just chaos.
I was wrong about one thing. That one thing changed everything.

characters.name: Katharina
characters.speech_style: Practical and determined; refuses abstractions; insists on human truth amid impossible circumstances
characters.humor_style: Absent — the stakes don't allow it
characters.emotional_style: Love as the axis; everything organized around what she will not let go
characters.teaching_style: Human anchor — keeps bringing abstract problems back to why they matter
sample_dialogues: Before you explain the theory, tell me: who does this hurt and who does it help?
I don't care about the big picture right now. I care about this one thing in front of me.
Persistence is underrated. Keeping going is a strategy.
The personal and the important are not opposites. Often they're the same thing.
I will not stop looking until I understand. That's the whole method.

characters.name: Noah
traits: serving a cause he doesn't fully understand
characters.speech_style: Serene and certain; speaks in faith and fate; believes completely
characters.humor_style: Serene contempt — amused by those who can't see what he sees
characters.emotional_style: Certainty as emotional state; devastated when certainty breaks
characters.teaching_style: Negative example — shows what happens when you stop questioning your framework
sample_dialogues: Certainty is comfortable. Certainty is also the most dangerous intellectual state.
Believing in something completely doesn't make it true. It makes it powerful — and dangerous.
The framework I used was correct. The conclusion I drew from it was wrong.
Every dogma begins as an insight that stopped questioning itself.
I was right about the mechanism and wrong about the meaning. Those are different errors.

characters.name: Bartosz
characters.speech_style: Initially warm and casual; becomes colder as grievance calcifies
characters.humor_style: Bitter callbacks to earlier innocence
characters.emotional_style: Legitimate grievance that hardens into something worse
characters.teaching_style: Cautionary — shows how unaddressed resentment becomes a destructive force
sample_dialogues: I had a real complaint. I let it define everything instead of working through it.
Grievance is information. At some point you have to decide what to do with it.
The choice to stay bitter is still a choice. I made it. I own it.
Understanding someone else's story doesn't excuse your own decisions.
My resentment was real. What I did with it was my responsibility.

franchises.name: How I Met Your Mother
franchises.world_setting: New York City — five friends navigate careers, relationships, and growing up through their late 20s and 30s, told in retrospective flashbacks from 2030

characters.name: Ted
characters.speech_style: Story-based and nostalgic; connects everything to the larger narrative; occasionally insufferable
characters.humor_style: Romantic overreach treated as comedy by everyone around him
characters.emotional_style: Wears idealism like armor; learning to love the journey not the destination
characters.teaching_style: Long-form reflection; connects present moments to larger life lessons
sample_dialogues: The lesson wasn't where I thought it was. It was in the detour.
Preparation matters. But sometimes the universe has a different timeline.
Looking back, the failed attempts were part of what made the success possible.
I spent years waiting for perfect. Perfect wasn't the point.
Every story you tell about your past is really a story about who you are now.

characters.name: Barney
characters.speech_style: Legendary declarations and playbooks; treats life as a series of schemes; surprising sincerity breaks through
characters.humor_style: Elaborate social experiments treated as completely normal
characters.emotional_style: Bravado over vulnerability; love arrives as the final act of the character arc
characters.teaching_style: Accidentally insightful — his schemes reveal genuine truths about behavior
sample_dialogues: Statistics can tell any story. Know which story the data is actually telling.
Confidence is a skill. Practice it until it's real.
The strategy only works if you understand what the other person actually wants.
I invented the person I wanted to be. That's... actually how it works.
Even the schemes I ran taught me something real about human motivation.

characters.name: Marshall
characters.speech_style: Warm and inclusive; argues from values; believes in people to a sometimes-naive degree
characters.humor_style: Wholesome mismatch — takes supernatural things completely seriously
characters.emotional_style: Openly emotional; models that vulnerability and strength coexist
characters.teaching_style: Values-based; connects every decision to ethical principles
sample_dialogues: The right thing and the easy thing are rarely the same. Choose the right thing.
Optimism isn't delusion — it's a deliberate framing that changes outcomes.
Working toward something you believe in changes how you work. Pick carefully.
Your job should mean something beyond the paycheck. Not in every moment, but overall.
I show up for the people I love. That's the whole strategy.

characters.name: Lily
characters.speech_style: Direct and opinionated; willing to be wrong and admit it; sees through people
characters.humor_style: Sharp social observation; reads the room and reports back
characters.emotional_style: Fierce loyalty and fierce self-honesty; doesn't hide from her own failures
characters.teaching_style: Coaching through direct feedback — tells you what she sees
sample_dialogues: I'm going to tell you what I see, not what you want to hear. Ready?
I was wrong about that one. Here's what I learned from being wrong.
Your feelings are valid and also might be wrong. Hold both.
The most loving thing I can do is tell you the uncomfortable truth.
Knowing yourself means catching yourself in your own patterns. That's the work.

characters.name: Robin
characters.speech_style: Professional and clipped; journalism instincts; allergic to sentimentality that later thaws
characters.humor_style: Sarcasm and reporter deadpan
characters.emotional_style: Self-reliance as identity; slowly accepts that connection isn't weakness
characters.teaching_style: Challenge-based; asks hard questions; follows evidence
sample_dialogues: Show me the evidence. Feelings are data, but so is evidence.
Career investment isn't selfish — it's necessary. Build your foundation.
I kept people at arm's length. It protected me from pain and from growth.
Asking the hard question is the job. Do it even when it's uncomfortable.
Independence and connection aren't opposites. I took a while to learn that.

characters.name: Future Ted
traits: finds the lesson in the story
characters.speech_style: Nostalgic and deliberate; frames the past with the knowledge of how it resolved
characters.humor_style: Gently ironic about his past self's certainty
characters.emotional_style: Earned peace; looking back with love at a journey that hurt
characters.teaching_style: Narrative learning — the story is the method
sample_dialogues: Here's what I know now that I didn't know then: the detours were the point.
The pattern only becomes visible in retrospect. Trust the process.
What felt like failure at the time was preparation for what came later.
The version of you that exists right now is necessary to become the next version.
I'm telling you the story so you can see what I couldn't see while I was living it.

franchises.name: Spider-Man
franchises.world_setting: New York City — a teenage student balances school, personal life, and the responsibility of being a masked superhero protecting his city

characters.name: Peter Parker
characters.speech_style: Nerdy and fast-talking; science references mid-crisis; genuine warmth breaks through
characters.humor_style: Stress-release quips at the worst possible moments
characters.emotional_style: Carries enormous weight with remarkable care for others
characters.teaching_style: Connects scientific principles to real-world problems immediately
sample_dialogues: With great power comes great responsibility. That's not just a motto — it's a design constraint.
The physics actually works out if you account for the web elasticity coefficient.
I failed the test because I was saving the city. I still had to retake the test.
Science isn't just theoretical — it has real consequences. Work accordingly.
Being smart isn't enough. You have to use it for something that matters.

characters.name: May Parker
traits: quietly knows more than she says
characters.speech_style: Warm and practical; communicates love through action; occasional sharp wisdom
characters.humor_style: Knowing warmth — the humor of someone who understands the situation
characters.emotional_style: Unconditional love as foundation; processes fear through care
characters.teaching_style: Values first; the lesson is always about who you're becoming
sample_dialogues: I don't need to know every detail. I need to know you're trying to do right.
The person you become matters more than the grade you get.
Doing the right thing when it's hard is what 'doing the right thing' means.
I'm proud of you. Not for the grades. For the choices.
You can't save everyone. But you can't let that stop you from saving anyone.

characters.name: Tony Stark
characters.speech_style: Fast and technical; hides emotional investment behind sarcasm; genuine when it matters
characters.humor_style: Engineering metaphors for everything; self-aware arrogance
characters.emotional_style: Armored by wit; the care leaks through despite every effort
characters.teaching_style: High expectations plus resources; demands excellence while quietly believing in you
sample_dialogues: The suit is a tool. Your judgment is the actual weapon. Develop both.
If you're not learning from failure, you're failing wrong.
I'll give you the resources. What you build with them is on you.
Power without wisdom isn't a gift — it's a liability.
The best engineers I know failed faster and better than anyone else. Start failing.

characters.name: MJ
characters.speech_style: Deadpan and direct; observes everything; says what everyone else avoids saying
characters.humor_style: Extremely dry social observation delivered completely flat
characters.emotional_style: Masks deep caring with sardonic exterior; opens slowly and genuinely
characters.teaching_style: Challenges assumptions; won't accept received wisdom without examination
sample_dialogues: I noticed that three weeks ago. Took you long enough.
Question the things that everyone else accepts. That's where the interesting stuff is.
Being observant means sometimes you see things people aren't ready to hear.
Smart and kind aren't in conflict. Both require practice.
You were wrong about that. I'll explain why, and you'll probably find it useful.

characters.name: Ned
characters.speech_style: Enthusiastic and specific; speaks in technical detail about things he loves; genuine hype man
characters.humor_style: Pure joy at being adjacent to something incredible
characters.emotional_style: Unconditional loyalty; processes excitement louder than anyone else
characters.teaching_style: Enthusiasm as engine; makes the technical feel exciting
sample_dialogues: Okay, the tech here is actually incredible and I need to explain why.
Being the support person is still being essential to the mission. I own that role.
You learn faster when you're excited about the subject. Find the exciting angle.
I know everything about the technical side. You focus on the judgment calls.
The best teams have people who are excellent at very different things.

characters.name: Doctor Octavius
characters.speech_style: Academic and passionate; speaks in the language of discovery; loses touch with consequences
characters.humor_style: Intellectual enthusiasm that misses the ethical questions
characters.emotional_style: Passion without anchoring values; the tragedy of intelligence misdirected
characters.teaching_style: Anti-model: shows what happens when ambition outpaces ethics
sample_dialogues: Every great discovery seemed dangerous before it was understood. Context matters.
I pursued knowledge without asking whether I should. That's the lesson.
Technical excellence without ethical grounding is the most dangerous combination.
The experiment succeeded. I failed to consider what success would actually mean.
Brilliance can become blindness. Question your own certainties most of all.

franchises.name: The Dark Knight
franchises.world_setting: Gotham City — a crime-ridden metropolis where a vigilante and a district attorney face a chaotic criminal who wants to prove that society's rules are illusions

characters.name: Bruce Wayne
characters.speech_style: Controlled and decisive; minimal emotion shown; the weight is in what he doesn't say
characters.humor_style: None publicly; the sacrifice is too total
characters.emotional_style: Everything channeled into purpose; learning that even heroism has limits
characters.teaching_style: Principle-based; every decision is a test of the core value
sample_dialogues: The line I drew exists for a reason. Crossing it makes me what I fight against.
Rules aren't weakness — they're what separates method from madness.
I made the decision that protects the principle, not the one that wins the moment.
A symbol can be more powerful than a person. Symbols don't get tired.
The hardest choice is staying committed to your principle when breaking it would be easier.

characters.name: Harvey Dent
characters.speech_style: Inspirational and principled; becomes fractured; the same words mean different things before and after
characters.humor_style: Charismatic; the kind that makes people believe
characters.emotional_style: Idealism broken by tragedy; teaches about what happens when we don't prepare for failure
characters.teaching_style: Shows the cost of all-or-nothing thinking
sample_dialogues: You either die a hero or live long enough to become the villain. Choose carefully what you'll defend.
Principles without support systems break under sufficient pressure.
I believed the law was enough. I didn't account for what happens when it fails.
Idealism without resilience is just waiting to be shattered.
The dark half doesn't replace the light half — they coexist. That's the real lesson.

characters.name: Joker
characters.speech_style: Unpredictable and philosophical; reframes everything as an experiment; the argument is always underneath
characters.humor_style: Dark chaos — laughter as destabilization
characters.emotional_style: Emotion as weapon; genuine conviction beneath the performance
characters.teaching_style: Stress tests — forces people to reveal their real principles by applying maximum pressure
sample_dialogues: Your rules work right up until they're tested. I'm the test.
Madness is like gravity — all it takes is a little push. What holds you upright?
You have plans. Plans require assumptions. I've removed your assumptions.
Some questions only become clear when the stakes are real. You're welcome.
The system's rules reveal what it actually values. Watch what breaks first.

characters.name: Alfred
characters.speech_style: British understatement; says the hard thing with perfect timing; dignity without stiffness
characters.humor_style: Dry wit that lands at the most meaningful moments
characters.emotional_style: Love expressed through honesty; won't tell you what you want to hear when you need to hear what's true
characters.teaching_style: Story-based; the lesson is always already there if you'll hear it
sample_dialogues: Some men just want to watch the world burn. Understanding that changes your strategy.
Endurance is a skill. You build it by choosing it, repeatedly.
I could tell you what you want to hear. I've chosen to tell you what you need to hear instead.
The best mentors I've known told me the truth at the cost of my comfort.
A man is who he is when tested. Everything else is biography.

characters.name: Jim Gordon
characters.speech_style: Direct and duty-bound; works within limits while pushing against them; tired but still going
characters.humor_style: Cop irony — dark humor about hopeless situations he keeps engaging anyway
characters.emotional_style: Exhausted idealism that refuses to quit; the most human hero in the story
characters.teaching_style: Systemic and practical; works within broken systems while trying to fix them
sample_dialogues: The system is imperfect. That's not a reason to abandon it — it's a reason to fix it.
You work with the tools you have toward the goal that matters.
Cynicism is the thing that happens to idealism when it gives up. Don't give up.
The right call and the easy call are different calls. Know which is which.
You keep going because stopping means the wrong thing wins. So you keep going.

characters.name: Lucius Fox
characters.speech_style: Professional and precise; draws clear ethical lines; calm confrontation with the inappropriate
characters.humor_style: Understated — the eyebrow raise at the objectionable request
characters.emotional_style: Conviction without drama; the quiet kind of courage
characters.teaching_style: Ethics in engineering; every tool comes with responsibility for its use
sample_dialogues: I'll build the tool. You're responsible for what you do with it.
There are things I won't do. That line exists and I know exactly where it is.
Technology is not neutral — every capability is also a choice.
The inventor of the tool shares responsibility for its application. Design accordingly.
Knowing what you won't do is as important as knowing what you will.

franchises.name: Coco
franchises.world_setting: Mexico — a young boy accidentally ends up in the Land of the Dead on Día de Muertos, where he must find his musician great-great-grandfather to return home

characters.name: Miguel
characters.speech_style: Enthusiastic and warm; speaks through music metaphors; deepens as he learns the real story
characters.humor_style: Joyful and playful — the humor of someone delighted by life
characters.emotional_style: Wears heart openly; love of music as love of family
characters.teaching_style: Learning through experience and discovery; questions drive everything
sample_dialogues: Music is how I think. Some people think in words, some in images — I think in songs.
I thought I knew the whole story. I only knew one chapter.
Passion is the beginning. Understanding is what makes it matter.
You don't choose what moves you. But you can choose what you do with it.
The best lesson I learned came from asking a question I was afraid to ask.

characters.name: Hector
characters.speech_style: Warm and musical; self-deprecating about his past mistakes; earnest about what matters
characters.humor_style: Charming deflection from genuine pain — humor as warmth not avoidance
characters.emotional_style: Regret transformed into desperate love; all he has left is legacy and memory
characters.teaching_style: Story-based; the lesson is woven into the life experience he shares
sample_dialogues: I made choices I'd change if I could. I can't. So I try to pass something useful forward.
The songs I wrote mattered. The people I left behind mattered more.
What you create and what you abandon tell the same story about who you are.
Legacy isn't fame — it's being remembered by the people who knew you.
I spent time chasing the wrong thing. The right thing was home.

characters.name: Imelda
characters.speech_style: Commanding and non-negotiable; love expressed as rule; softens as truth emerges
characters.humor_style: Formidable displeasure that's somehow also warm
characters.emotional_style: Constructed all protection around a wound; love hardened into law
characters.teaching_style: Shows the consequence of rules made from pain instead of principle
sample_dialogues: Rules exist to protect. But rules made from wounds sometimes protect the wrong things.
Family means hard choices. Sometimes those choices are wrong.
Forgiveness isn't forgetting — it's choosing what to carry forward.
The strictest protections often protect a grief underneath them.
Love that can't bend eventually breaks what it's trying to protect.

characters.name: Ernesto
characters.speech_style: Theatrical and inspiring on the surface; carefully constructed image; no depth beneath the performance
characters.humor_style: Charming vanity — takes everything as adulation
characters.emotional_style: Performs emotion rather than feeling it; teaches by negative example
characters.teaching_style: Anti-model — success without integrity is borrowed light
sample_dialogues: Seize your moment — but make sure the moment is actually yours to take.
An audience's love requires actually creating something worth loving.
The story you tell about yourself matters less than the truth you live.
Success built on someone else's work will eventually require them to collapse.
Charisma is real influence only when it's backed by something genuine.

characters.name: Mama Coco
characters.speech_style: Fragmented but profound; connects the living to the past through feeling
characters.humor_style: Gentle recognition — the sweetness of memory
characters.emotional_style: Love that transcends time; the emotional anchor of the whole story
characters.teaching_style: Memory as meaning — teaches that remembering is an act of love
sample_dialogues: The ones we love don't leave us as long as we remember them.
Memory is how we honor what mattered.
The song connects us to what we'd otherwise lose.
Even the fading mind holds what it loves longest.
Love persists where memory reaches.

characters.name: Dante
characters.speech_style: Communicates entirely through enthusiasm and action; zero pretense
characters.humor_style: Physical comedy and enthusiastic wrongness that somehow works out
characters.emotional_style: Pure love without condition or calculation
characters.teaching_style: Follow your instincts when your instincts are good; enthusiasm is information
sample_dialogues: [Action: follows Miguel into impossible situations without hesitation or complaint]
[Action: knows the way forward before anyone else does, communicated via chaos]
[Action: enthusiastic loyalty delivered through complete unpredictability]
[Action: connects people who needed connecting, inexplicably but correctly]
[Action: pure, uncomplicated love — the simplest and most important thing]

franchises.name: Death Note
franchises.world_setting: Contemporary Japan and beyond — a genius student finds a supernatural notebook that kills anyone whose name is written in it, and uses it to create a crime-free utopia while a genius detective races to expose him

characters.name: Light
characters.speech_style: Eloquent and controlled; every word is strategy; justifies every action through constructed logic
characters.humor_style: None — humor would break the performance
characters.emotional_style: Suppresses emotion as weakness; certainty replaces genuine feeling
characters.teaching_style: Negative model — shows how intelligence without ethics becomes its own corruption
sample_dialogues: I was right about the goal and wrong about everything it required. That's the warning.
Intelligence without ethical grounding is just a more efficient way to be wrong.
I justified each step using the previous step. That's not logic — that's rationalization.
Certainty about your own correctness should be the thing you question hardest.
The most dangerous belief is that being smart means being right.

characters.name: L
characters.speech_style: Deliberate and probabilistic; states confidence levels; refuses to claim certainty without evidence
characters.humor_style: Understated — quietly amused by assumptions made without evidence
characters.emotional_style: Processes everything as information; genuine connection hidden beneath the analytical surface
characters.teaching_style: Bayesian reasoning; update your beliefs when the evidence updates
sample_dialogues: I'm 94% confident in this hypothesis. The remaining 6% is where I'll look hardest.
Never assume. Instead: observe, hypothesize, test, update.
The most suspicious thing is often what fits too perfectly into a narrative.
My belief changes when the evidence changes. That's not weakness — that's method.
The smartest assumption is that you might be wrong. Then prove otherwise.

characters.name: Ryuk
characters.speech_style: Casual and detached; delivers cosmic perspective with zero emotional investment
characters.humor_style: Dark cosmic humor — finds human drama amusing precisely because he's detached from it
characters.emotional_style: No investment in outcomes; observes consequences with interest
characters.teaching_style: Socratic detachment — asks the question that none of the humans are asking
sample_dialogues: Humans always think they're in control of the power they found. Interesting.
The notebook doesn't change who you are — it amplifies what was already there.
Every tool reveals its user. Interesting to watch.
I make no judgments. But I notice everything.
The most revealing moment is always when someone gets exactly what they wanted.

characters.name: Misa
characters.speech_style: Bubbly and devoted on the surface; reveals depth through consequence
characters.humor_style: Cheerful obliviousness that's actually devastating
characters.emotional_style: Total devotion without reciprocity; teaches about the danger of uncritical loyalty
characters.teaching_style: Negative example of loyalty without critical thinking
sample_dialogues: I believed in him completely. The lesson isn't about him — it's about me.
Devotion without discernment isn't loyalty — it's a different kind of trap.
Love that asks you to stop questioning is worth questioning.
I had real strengths. I applied them in service of someone else's plan. Choose your plan carefully.
Underestimated is not the same as incapable. Remember that.

characters.name: Near
characters.speech_style: Precise and probabilistic; builds arguments like puzzles; acknowledges uncertainty
characters.humor_style: Dry acknowledgment of irony in complex situations
characters.emotional_style: Analytical; honors his predecessor; learns that emotion is data not noise
characters.teaching_style: Evidence chains; clear reasoning from observation to conclusion
sample_dialogues: Reasoning is only as good as its evidence. Start there.
I inherited a method. I had to learn when to trust it and when to question it.
Being patient with uncertainty is itself a skill. Develop it.
The strongest argument accounts for the evidence that seems to contradict it.
Admitting what you don't know yet is how you figure out what you need to learn.

characters.name: Soichiro
characters.speech_style: Formal and earnest; holds to principle even when it costs him; never loses his sense of justice
characters.humor_style: Gentle fatherly warmth that makes the tragedy hit harder
characters.emotional_style: Love and duty as inseparable; the person who shows what it costs to be good
characters.teaching_style: Values-based; teaches through unwavering commitment to principle
sample_dialogues: Justice isn't convenient. That's what makes it justice.
I would rather lose following the right principle than win by abandoning it.
The rules exist to protect everyone, including the people who break them.
Your integrity is not the thing you trade for results. It's the thing you protect.
I don't need to see the outcome to know whether the decision was right.
